rules.statement: All Python source files under src/ MUST reside within a constitutional layer (mind/, body/, will/), a sanctioned infrastructure directory (shared/, api/), or be a root entry point. No other top-level directories are permitted under src/.
rules.rationale: The constitution defines exactly three layers: Mind, Body, Will — plus shared infrastructure and API entrypoints. Code that exists outside these boundaries is ungoverned by definition. This rule closes the perimeter so that no shadow layers can accumulate undetected.

rules.statement: Mind layer components (src/mind/**/*.py) MUST NOT import database session infrastructure (get_session)
rules.rationale: Mind defines law but never executes. Database access is execution, not governance.

rules.statement: Mind layer components (src/mind/**/*.py) MUST NOT write to filesystem
rules.rationale: Mind may read .intent/ but never mutates. Law does not modify itself.

rules.statement: Mind layer components (src/mind/**/*.py) MUST NOT import or invoke Body layer (src/body)
rules.rationale: Mind defines rules. Body executes. Mind never invokes execution.

rules.statement: Mind layer components (src/mind/**/*.py) MUST NOT import or invoke Will layer (src/will)
rules.rationale: Mind defines rules. Will decides. Mind never makes decisions.

rules.statement: Body layer components (src/body/**/*.py) MUST NOT evaluate constitutional rules directly
rules.rationale: Body executes actions. Mind evaluates rules. Body never decides legality.

rules.statement: Will layer components (src/will/**/*.py) MUST NOT import get_session directly
rules.rationale: Will decides and orchestrates. Body executes. Will delegates infrastructure access to Body.

rules.statement: Will layer components (src/will/**/*.py) SHOULD delegate filesystem operations to Body
rules.rationale: Will may read for context but should not implement filesystem operations directly.

rules.statement: RETIRED from automated enforcement (#820 Group B). Its currently mechanically decidable portions are covered by architecture.will.no_direct_database_access and architecture.will.no_filesystem_operations, which forbid Will from touching those two infrastructure surfaces directly. This rule no longer dispatches to a mapping. Historical marker only — see rationale.
rules.rationale: This rule's check_type (component_responsibility, engine=knowledge_gate) was never implemented by any engine — inert since it was mapped, the same #820 dispatch-integrity defect closed in Group A for capability.taxonomy.*. Its own statement was a positive routing claim ('SHOULD import and delegate to Body services'), materially harder to verify mechanically than a forbidden-pattern check: proving delegation occurred requires call-graph or semantic analysis no CORE engine performs, whereas proving the ABSENCE of a direct database or filesystem call is exactly what architecture.will.no_direct_database_access and architecture.will.no_filesystem_operations already do. Those two rules are the mechanical coverage for this claim's currently decidable portions only, not a replacement for the full claim. The residual — that Will's business logic is actually implemented via Body delegation, not merely avoiding those two specific bypasses — is not exhaustively decided by CORE's current declared checks and remains subject to governor/human architecture review. Retired rather than deleted so the prior declaration stays traceable. Its mapping entry and remediation-map entry are removed.

rules.statement: API layer components (src/api/**/*.py) MUST NOT import get_session directly
rules.rationale: API is entrypoint boundary. All work must route through Will, not directly to infrastructure.

rules.statement: RETIRED from automated enforcement (#820 Group B). Its currently mechanically decidable portions are covered by architecture.api.no_direct_database_access and architecture.api.no_body_bypass, which forbid API from bypassing to those two layers directly. This rule no longer dispatches to a mapping. Historical marker only — see rationale.
rules.rationale: This rule's check_type (component_responsibility, engine=knowledge_gate) was never implemented by any engine — inert since it was mapped, the same #820 dispatch-integrity defect closed in Group A for capability.taxonomy.*. Its own statement was a positive routing claim ('SHOULD delegate all logic to Will layer'), materially harder to verify mechanically than a forbidden-pattern check: proving all logic routes through Will requires call-graph or semantic analysis no CORE engine performs, whereas proving the ABSENCE of a direct database or Body-bypass import is exactly what architecture.api.no_direct_database_access and architecture.api.no_body_bypass already do. Those two rules are the mechanical coverage for this claim's currently decidable portions only, not a replacement for the full claim. The residual — a genuine Will use-case layer that API always routes through — remains aspirational per CORE-Mind-Body-Will-Separation.md §6 and ADR-049, not exhaustively decided by CORE's current declared checks, and is tracked as architectural debt reviewed by governor/human judgment rather than an automated check. Retired rather than deleted so the prior declaration stays traceable. Its mapping entry and remediation-map entry are removed.

rules.statement: API layer components SHOULD NOT directly import Body services
rules.rationale: API should route through Will, not bypass to Body directly.

rules.statement: Body layer components (src/body/**/*.py) MUST NOT import or invoke Will layer (src/will)
rules.rationale: Body executes. Will decides. Execution never chooses strategy.

rules.statement: RETIRED from automated enforcement (#820 Group B). Its currently mechanically decidable portions are covered by architecture.mind.no_database_access, architecture.mind.no_filesystem_writes, architecture.mind.no_body_invocation, and architecture.mind.no_will_invocation. This rule no longer dispatches to a mapping. Historical marker only — see rationale.
rules.rationale: This rule's check_type (component_responsibility, engine=knowledge_gate) was never implemented by any engine — it dispatched to nothing and reported clean on every audit, the same #820 dispatch-integrity defect closed for the capability.taxonomy.* rules in Group A. Rather than implement a new mechanism for a broad umbrella claim, the four sibling rules already listed above mechanically cover the currently decidable portions of its surface: database access, filesystem writes, invoking Body, invoking Will — each a real ast_gate import-boundary check. The residual claim this rule made beyond those four — that Mind performs no I/O or action-invocation of any kind, including forms the four siblings do not enumerate (e.g. network I/O) — is not exhaustively decided by CORE's current declared checks and remains an open architectural expectation reviewed by governor/human judgment, not a gate. Retired rather than deleted so the prior declaration stays traceable. Its mapping entry and remediation-map entry are removed.

rules.statement: Shared infrastructure components (src/shared/**/*.py) MUST NOT make strategic decisions or orchestrate workflows. This is Test 2 of the CORE-Mind-Body-Will-Separation.md §7.2 admission test — retained as normative architectural doctrine, evaluated by governor/human architecture review, not by an automated mechanical check.
rules.rationale: Whether a piece of shared code 'makes a strategic decision' is a semantic judgment, not a pattern a deterministic engine can verify — no check_type exists or is planned for this (#820 Group B declines to invent one). This rule's prior mapping (component_responsibility, engine=knowledge_gate) was never implemented and dispatched to nothing; it is removed rather than left declared-but-inert. The mechanical half of the admission test — Test 1, layer independence — is enforced separately and for real by architecture.shared.no_layer_imports (blocking ast_gate). This rule covers Test 2 only, and remains advisory doctrine: a real constitutional expectation that binds by architecture review, not by gate. CORE-Mind-Body-Will-Separation.md §7.6 previously and incorrectly claimed this rule had been upgraded to a blocking AST gate; that claim is corrected there.

rules.statement: Shared infrastructure components (src/shared/**/*.py) MUST NOT import from src/mind/, src/body/, or src/will/.
rules.rationale: Shared is layer-independent substrate. A shared module that imports from a layer fails the constitutional admission test (CORE-Mind-Body-Will-Separation.md §7.2 Test 1) and belongs in that layer, not in shared/. This rule makes the admission test mechanically enforceable. Added 2026-03-28 as a blocking AST gate, replacing previously advisory-only governance.

rules.statement: Workers in src/will/workers/ and src/body/workers/ MUST NOT directly import from other modules within those directories at runtime. All inter-worker communication routes exclusively through the blackboard (post_finding, post_report, post_heartbeat). Utility functions shared between workers belong in shared/ or in a non-workers Will subdirectory.
rules.rationale: Direct worker-to-worker imports create structural coupling that makes it possible for one Worker to call another Worker's methods directly, bypassing the blackboard-only communication contract. The constitutional guarantee that every Worker action is recorded on the blackboard is only enforceable if workers cannot obtain references to other worker instances via import. Existing helper modules co-located in will/workers/ are excluded pending extraction to shared/; no new cross-worker imports are permitted.

rules.statement: Every APIRouter module declaring ROUTER_EXPOSURE="governor-only" MUST carry require_governor in its APIRouter constructor dependencies list. No APIRouter module declaring ROUTER_EXPOSURE="user-facing" MAY carry require_governor at the router constructor level (per-route gates are permitted).
rules.rationale: ROUTER_EXPOSURE is the machine-readable declaration of who may call a route. If the enforcement dependency does not match the declaration, the declaration is lying — a governed route can be called without the required authority, or an unintended restriction blocks legitimate callers. This rule ensures ROUTER_EXPOSURE is never decorative. Introduced by ADR-132 D7.

rules.statement: Every *_routes.py module in src/api/v1/ MUST declare a ROUTER_EXPOSURE module-level assignment with a value from the closed set {'governor-only', 'user-facing'}.
rules.rationale: Completeness companion to router_exposure_must_match_dependencies. The consistency rule (ADR-132 D7) only fires when ROUTER_EXPOSURE is present; a new route file that silently omits the declaration escapes classification entirely. This rule closes that gap: any *_routes.py file without ROUTER_EXPOSURE is undeclared and therefore ungoverned.

rules.statement: Every POST/PUT/DELETE/PATCH route declared on a ROUTER_EXPOSURE='user-facing' module MUST carry require_governor, either in the route decorator's dependencies list or as a handler parameter default, unless explicitly confirmed as intentionally ungated.
rules.rationale: router_exposure_must_match_dependencies only validates the router-CONSTRUCTOR-level gate; it has no visibility into per-route decorators, so a newly added sensitive endpoint on an already-compliant user-facing router ships completely ungated and that rule still passes. This is the per-route completeness companion, mirroring route_module_must_declare_exposure's relationship to the consistency rule. Sensitivity signal is the HTTP mutation verb, reusing the read/write axis CORE already applies elsewhere (ActionImpact, action_risk.yaml) rather than a new taxonomy. Reporting rather than blocking: require_governor is a no-op passthrough in this OSS build (api/dependencies.py), so the gap is currently structural rather than live-exploitable, and an initial sweep surfaces a nonzero false-positive set (read-shaped POST routes) that should be triaged before promotion to blocking (#770).
